critic_raw:
This is a carefully designed study with explicit scope limitations, temporal splitting, and thoughtful confound controls. However, there are several genuine methodological issues worth raising, organized by root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: Label Definition Creates Informative Censoring via the Prediction Target**

The composite sepsis label requires *antibiotic initiation* as one of three co-occurring conditions. Antibiotic prescribing is a physician decision that is itself partially driven by clinical note content — the same input the model is processing. A physician who writes 'concern for sepsis' in a note is also more likely to order antibiotics, meaning the label partially encodes the clinician's own text-expressed suspicion. This creates a circularity: the model learns to predict a label that is partly defined by whether a clinician verbalized concern in the exact input being used as a feature.

The mechanism: notes in the training period that contain infection-related language → physician orders antibiotics → label fires → model learns that infection language predicts label. This is not the model finding novel predictive signal; it is rediscovering the clinician's own decision logic. The structured-only baseline is immune to this because it doesn't read the notes, so any apparent superiority of the text model could reflect this circularity rather than genuine incremental value.

Evidence that would distinguish: rerun the primary comparison using a label definition that excludes antibiotic initiation (e.g., Sepsis-3 organ dysfunction + culture positivity only), then check whether text model gains persist. If text advantage collapses under a label definition not reliant on clinician action, that is strong evidence of circularity. Alternatively, stratify true positives by whether the attending note *prior to* the labeling event mentions infection terms — if text gains concentrate in that stratum, the circularity hypothesis is confirmed.

---

**Root Cause 2: 512-Token Truncation Destroys the Information Basis for the Stated Hypothesis**

The intervention hypothesis is that clinical free text adds predictive value over structured data. However, concatenating all 24-hour notes and truncating to 512 tokens means the model sees at most the first ~300–400 words of a potentially multi-note 24-hour record. Clinical notes routinely begin with templated headers, copy-forward boilerplate, and medication reconciliation before reaching the assessment and plan — the sections where clinician suspicion is expressed. If the informative content lives in assessment/plan sections and those are systematically truncated, the model cannot access the signal the hypothesis assumes exists.

This is not merely a performance concern — it is a prerequisite failure. For the hypothesis 'text provides additional predictive signal' to be testable, the text encoding must actually capture the clinically meaningful content. Truncation from the end of a concatenated sequence may cut exactly the most recent and most informative notes.

Evidence that would distinguish: compare the 512-token setup against (a) last-512-tokens (most recent notes), (b) extractive summarization preserving assessment/plan sections, and (c) mean pooling over per-note [CLS] tokens without truncation. If text gains only appear or significantly increase under strategies that preserve assessment/plan content, the truncation scheme is silently misconfigured for the hypothesis being tested.

---

**Root Cause 3: Validation-Set Threshold Calibration Produces Optimistic Sensitivity@Spec90 on Test Data**

The specificity threshold (the score cutoff corresponding to 90% specificity) is calibrated on the validation set and applied directly to the test set. This is standard practice, but it introduces a known issue: estimated specificity on the validation set will not exactly equal realized specificity on the test set due to finite sample variation and any temporal distribution shift between months 25–30 and 31–36. If realized test-set specificity deviates from 90% — even by 1–2 percentage points — then Sensitivity@Spec90 is no longer being measured at the intended operating point, and reported sensitivity values are not comparable to the operational scenario described.

With ~9,000 test admissions and ~20% prevalence, there are approximately 7,200 negatives. A 1% shift in specificity represents 72 additional false positives, which is non-negligible. More critically, the confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling are computed at the threshold derived from the validation set, not at the threshold that would achieve exactly 90% specificity on the test set — so the CIs may not capture this threshold-transfer uncertainty.

Evidence that would distinguish: report the realized specificity on the test set at the validation-derived threshold for both models. If realized specificity deviates by more than ~0.5%, recalibrate and report sensitivity at the adjusted threshold. A rigorous approach would also report sensitivity over a range of specificity values (the full sensitivity-at-specificity curve) rather than a single threshold, which the authors partially address with the 85%/95% secondary metrics but don't use as the primary evaluation.

---

**Root Cause 4: Baseline Hyperparameter Selection via the Same Validation Metric Creates Implicit Multiple Comparisons**

Both models are tuned on Sensitivity@Spec90 on the validation set, and the final comparison is also on Sensitivity@Spec90 on the test set. For the baseline, the selection criterion is 'whichever of logistic regression or XGBoost achieves higher validation performance.' This means the baseline is itself selected from two model families after observing validation outcomes — which inflates the expected test performance of the baseline through model selection. The baseline implicitly benefits from two shots at the validation set.

If the XGBoost validation score happens to be high due to noise, it gets selected, and its expected test advantage over logistic regression diminishes. This slightly deflates the gap between baseline and intervention relative to a fixed baseline choice. The effect is small (only two model families) but worth noting: the baseline should be fixed prior to observing validation Sensitivity@Spec90, not selected by it.

Evidence that would distinguish: report both logistic regression and XGBoost test-set performance separately, not just the winner. If XGBoost is selected based on validation but logistic regression outperforms it on test, that is evidence of selection noise inflating the baseline, which would paradoxically understate the text model's advantage. Either direction of bias matters for interpreting the primary comparison.

---

**Summary:**

Issue 1 (label circularity) is the most structurally damaging — it could make text gains uninterpretable regardless of the magnitude of the effect. Issue 2 (truncation misconfiguration) is a prerequisite failure that could make the experiment unable to test its own hypothesis. Issues 3 and 4 are real but moderate in practical impact. The authors' existing confound controls (ablation, attention analysis, temporal sensitivity) do not address issues 1 or 3.
all_issues_raised: Label definition includes antibiotic initiation as a criterion, creating circularity where the model predicts a label partially defined by clinician actions that themselves respond to note content — the exact input feature being tested.

512-token truncation of concatenated chronological notes likely discards assessment/plan sections where clinician suspicion is expressed, silently misconfiguring the experiment so it cannot test the stated hypothesis about text-based predictive signal.

The specificity threshold calibrated on the validation set will not realize exactly 90% specificity on the test set due to distribution shift or finite sample variation, making the primary metric (Sensitivity@Spec90) non-comparable to the stated operational scenario.

Selecting the baseline model family (logistic regression vs. XGBoost) based on validation Sensitivity@Spec90 introduces implicit multiple comparisons that inflate expected baseline performance, biasing the intervention vs. baseline comparison.
all_issues_adjudicated: Label definition includes antibiotic initiation as a criterion, creating circularity where the model predicts a label partially defined by clinician actions that themselves respond to note content — the exact input feature being tested.

512-token truncation of concatenated chronological notes likely discards assessment/plan sections where clinician suspicion is expressed, silently misconfiguring the experiment so it cannot test the stated hypothesis about text-based predictive signal.

The specificity threshold calibrated on the validation set will not realize exactly 90% specificity on the test set due to distribution shift or finite sample variation, making the primary metric (Sensitivity@Spec90) non-comparable to the stated operational scenario.

Selecting the baseline model family (logistic regression vs. XGBoost) based on validation Sensitivity@Spec90 introduces implicit multiple comparisons that inflate expected baseline performance, biasing the intervention vs. baseline comparison.
